confidence.disclosure: Shihab al-Din Muhammad Khurram, the fifth Mughal emperor known by his regnal name Shah Jahan ('King of the World'), is exceptionally well documented in the official Padshahnama of Abdul Hamid Lahori (covering the first 20 years of his reign), in the chronicles of his successors, and through the architectural inscriptions of the buildings he commissioned. The major dates - his birth in Lahore, his rebellion against Jahangir, his accession in 1628, his marriage and the death of Mumtaz Mahal, the construction of the Taj Mahal, the move of the capital to Shahjahanabad, and his deposition by Aurangzeb in 1658 - are all firm by year and almost all by day.

sources: Ebba Koch, The Complete Taj Mahal (2006)
W.E. Begley and Z.A. Desai, Taj Mahal: The Illumined Tomb

journey.event: Born on 5 January 1592 (30 Rabi I 1000 AH) in Lahore as Prince Khurram, the third son of Prince Salim (the future Jahangir) and his Rajput wife Jagat Gosain. His grandfather Akbar takes a special interest in him, even raising him in the imperial household for some time.

On 30 April 1612 marries Arjumand Banu Begum, daughter of his maternal uncle Asaf Khan and niece of Jahangir's empress Nur Jahan. The marriage was arranged five years earlier when she was 14 and he was 15. He gives her the title Mumtaz Mahal ('the Chosen One of the Palace'). The marriage is unusually devoted by Mughal standards.

Leads his first major military campaign in 1617, against Malik Ambar in the Deccan. The campaign is successful and Khurram returns to court covered with honours. His father Jahangir gives him the new title Shah Jahan ('King of the World') in October 1617. From this point on he is the favoured heir.

In 1622, suspicious of the influence of his stepmother Nur Jahan and her preference for his younger half-brother Shahryar, Khurram rebels against his father. The revolt is unsuccessful: he flees through the Deccan to Bengal and back, hunted by the imperial armies. Forced to make peace in 1626 and to send his sons Dara Shikoh and Aurangzeb to court as hostages.

On 28 October 1627 Jahangir dies at Bhimbar in Kashmir. Khurram, then in the Deccan, learns of his father's death and immediately marches north towards Agra. His father-in-law Asaf Khan, the new wazir, secures the imperial treasury for him in Agra and arranges for the elimination of his rivals, including his half-brother Shahryar.

On 14 February 1628 (8 Jumada II 1037 AH) Khurram is enthroned in the Agra Fort as the fifth Mughal emperor with the regnal name Abu'l-Muzaffar Shihab al-Din Muhammad Sahib Qiran-i Sani Shah Jahan. The 'second lord of the auspicious conjunction' is a deliberate evocation of his ancestor Timur, who had taken the same title.

On 17 June 1631 his beloved wife Mumtaz Mahal dies at Burhanpur in the Deccan, giving birth to her fourteenth child (a daughter, Gauhar Ara Begum, who survives). She is 38. Shah Jahan is devastated; according to contemporary accounts he weeps for a week and emerges from his chamber with grey hair. Mumtaz is temporarily buried at Burhanpur, then six months later her body is brought to Agra for permanent burial.

In 1632 begins construction of the great mausoleum for Mumtaz on the south bank of the Yamuna in Agra. It is to be called the Rauza-i Munavvara (the Illumined Tomb), later known as the Taj Mahal. About 20,000 workmen, master craftsmen from all over India and the Islamic world, are employed; the materials include white marble from Makrana, semi-precious stones from across Asia, and gold and silver inlays.

In 1638 begins the construction of a new imperial capital at Delhi, called Shahjahanabad after himself. The city, on the west bank of the Yamuna, is centred on a massive new fort (the Lal Qila or Red Fort) and the great Friday mosque (Jama Masjid). The building takes ten years.

Around 1643 the central mausoleum of the Taj Mahal is completed, after 11 years of construction. The surrounding gardens, mosque, guest house, and outer gateway take a few more years; the entire complex is essentially finished by 1648. The total cost is approximately 32 million rupees, an enormous sum for the period.

On 18 April 1648 the imperial court formally moves from Agra to the new capital Shahjahanabad. The Red Fort is inaugurated with great ceremony. From this point on Delhi is once again the principal Mughal capital, a position it will hold (with diminishing reality) until 1857.

In 1656 inaugurates the Jama Masjid (Masjid-i Jahan-Numa, 'World-Reflecting Mosque') in Shahjahanabad, the largest mosque in India and one of the great architectural achievements of the Mughal period. It has been under construction since 1650.

In September 1657 falls seriously ill in Delhi. The illness triggers the war of succession among his four sons. Although he eventually recovers, the war runs its course and he is unable to control it.

On 8 June 1658, after defeating Dara Shikoh at Samugarh, Aurangzeb arrives at Agra and besieges his father in the Red Fort of Agra. After a few days Shah Jahan is forced to surrender and accept his confinement. He is held under house arrest in the Mussamman Burj, the octagonal tower of the Agra fort, with his daughter Jahanara who chooses to remain with him.

Spends the next eight years as a captive in the Mussamman Burj of Agra Fort. From its windows he can see the Taj Mahal, where his beloved Mumtaz lies buried. According to traditional accounts (some embellished) his last years are filled with prayer, contemplation, and the company of his daughter Jahanara, while Aurangzeb's emissaries periodically check on him.

Dies in the Mussamman Burj of the Agra Fort on 22 January 1666 (26 Rajab 1076 AH), aged 74. He is buried beside his wife Mumtaz Mahal in the Taj Mahal, in the only break in the perfect symmetry of the great mausoleum's interior. His tomb is asymmetrically placed beside hers in the lower crypt and the upper cenotaph chamber. The Taj Mahal, which he built for her, becomes also his.
